message.content:
You are an independent, adversarial fix-confirmation reviewer (reviewer A) for the hc-assay production-hardening campaign, repo at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-7).

Consolidated operator-identity PII remediation in commit 7c50440 on branch harden/pass-7, addressing BOTH:
(a) J-002 confirm-concern: the prior full-name scrub ("[REDACTED]") left the operator's BARE first/last name in cleartext governance prose (256x "[REDACTED] approves ...", "[REDACTED] builds and governs ...").
(b) J-008 (new, caught by the pass-7 privacy coverage matrix): the operator GitHub handle "[REDACTED]" leaked across 193 transcript files (github.com/<handle> URLs, "gh --repo <handle>/..." commands) — PII no email rule (needs @) or username rule (home dir is "[REDACTED]") covered.

THE FIX (scripts/capture_transcripts.py):
1. _display_name_patterns(): in addition to the full-name pattern, now also emits a per-TOKEN pattern for each name token with len>=4 (e.g. "[REDACTED]\b", "[REDACTED]\b") — NO leading \b (handles JSON-\n-glued names), trailing \b (protects "Joneses"). So the bare first/last name is redacted standalone.
2. _repo_handle_patterns() (NEW): derives the repo owner from "git remote -v" (regex github.com[:/]<owner>/) + the ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES env; redacts each handle (len>=4) as a bare token everywhere (URLs, gh args, <handle>@ email stem). Repo name is preserved (legitimate provenance).
3. Both applied in _scrub after the username rule.
4. The committed corpus was RE-SCRUBBED in place (207 files rewritten).
Regression tests: test_scrub_redacts_bare_name_tokens, test_scrub_redacts_github_handle (in tests/test_scripts.py).

Read the actual changed file /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py and the tests. You may run tests: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -m pytest -q tests/test_scripts.py  (use "python -m pytest", NOT bare pytest).

CRITICALLY, independently verify the COMMITTED CORPUS is actually clean — run grep yourself, e.g.:
  cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -rl "[REDACTED]" transcripts/ | wc -l
  cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -rl "[REDACTED]" transcripts/ | wc -l
  cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -rloE "[REDACTED][^e]" transcripts/ | wc -l
A passing unit test does NOT prove the committed transcripts are clean — verify both.

Decide:
- appropriate: right root cause, right layer, sound approach, acceptable over-redaction trade-off (the corpus has no unrelated bearer of "[REDACTED]"/"[REDACTED]"; confirm or refute by sampling contexts).
- complete: corpus actually clean (grep ~0 for operator name tokens + handle, ignoring the bare provider word "protonmail" in meta-discussion which is not PII), regression tests discriminate (would fail if reverted), and no obvious remaining operator-identity vector.
Return CONFIRMED only if both true. CONCERN if appropriate but a real residual gap remains (name a concrete one). REJECTED if wrong/non-discriminating. List concrete concerns; empty array if none.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll independently verify this fix. Let me start by reading the changed file and tests, then verify the corpus.

content.content:
1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""Capture the active Claude session's JSONL transcript(s) into the repo for provenance.
3	
4	Run by the pre-commit hook (.githooks/pre-commit) so every commit pulls in an up-to-date
5	snapshot of the agent interactions/effort behind it — an auditable record alongside the work.
6	
7	What it captures: the *active* session (the most-recently-modified top-level transcript in the
8	Claude project dir) plus that session's ``<id>/`` subtree (subagents + workflow transcripts).
9	Other/unrelated sessions are left out. Credential-shaped secrets are redacted on the way in.
10	
11	Honest scope: transcripts still contain local filesystem paths and may contain the operator's
12	email and internal reasoning. If this repo is published, review/exclude ``transcripts/`` first
13	(see transcripts/README.md). The wheel never ships them (only ``src/assay_engine`` is packaged).
14	
15	Config: ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC overrides the source dir. Exits 0 on any error (never blocks a
16	commit).
17	"""
18	
19	from __future__ import annotations
20	
21	import datetime as _dt
22	import hashlib
23	import json
24	import os
25	import re
26	import sys
27	from pathlib import Path
28	
29	DEFAULT_SRC = Path.home() / ".claude" / "projects" / "-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc"
30	DEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "sessions"
31	MANIFEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "MANIFEST.json"
32	
33	# Credential-shaped secrets to redact (defense). Pass 3 (#F-024) extends this to operator PII —
34	# email addresses and the home-directory username embedded in filesystem paths — so committing
35	# transcripts as provenance (the campaign mandate) does not also publish the operator's identity.
36	# Reasoning content and the path STRUCTURE remain (legitimate provenance); see the README caveat
37	# for the full public-release consideration.
38	_SECRET_PATTERNS = [
39	    re.compile(r"sk-ant-[A-Za-z0-9_-]{2,}"),  # any sk-ant- token incl. oat01 OAuth + placeholders
40	    re.compile(r"\bgh[posru]_[A-Za-z0-9]{20,}\b"),  # GitHub tokens
41	    re.compile(r"\bAKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}\b"),  # AWS access key id
42	    # bearer tokens incl. base64url/base64 JWTs — must cover + / = or the token is split at the
43	    # first such char and only the leading fragment is redacted (pass 3, #F-051).
44	    re.compile(r"(?i)bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-+/=]{20,}"),
45	    # explicit OAuth/token env values: "...OAUTH_TOKEN":"<value>" / ...KEY=<value>
46	    re.compile(r'((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)"\s*:\s*")[^"]{8,}(")'),
47	    re.compile(r"((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)=)[^\s\"']{8,}"),
48	    # operator PII (#F-024): email addresses, and the username segment of a home path
49	    # (/Users/[REDACTED] /home/[REDACTED] — the path STRUCTURE is kept, only the identity is masked.
50	    re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}\b"),  # email → [REDACTED]
51	    re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+"),  # /Users/[REDACTED] → /Users/[REDACTED]    # The Claude project-dir SLUG (e.g. -Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc): redact the username segment
53	    # regardless of length (#H-018) — the bare-token rule below skips short handles to avoid
54	    # over-redacting common words, but the slug form is unambiguous PII at any length.
55	    re.compile(r"(-(?:Users|home)-)[^-\s/\"']+"),  # -Users-[REDACTED]- → -Users-[REDACTED]-
56	]
57	
58	
59	def _username_pattern() -> re.Pattern[str] | None:
60	    """A redaction for the bare operator username wherever it appears (#G-007).
61	
62	    The /Users|/home path rule only masks the username inside a path; the same handle leaks via
63	    git author strings, prose, and the Claude project-dir slug (``-Users-[REDACTED]-...``). Derive the
64	    handle from the home directory and redact it as a standalone token too. Skipped for trivially
65	    short/None handles (e.g. a CI 'root') to avoid over-redacting common words.
66	    """
67	    try:
68	        name = Path.home().name
69	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - never break capture over a missing home
70	        return None
71	    if not name or len(name) < 4:
72	        return None
73	    return re.compile(re.escape(name))
74	
75	
76	def _display_name_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
77	    """Redactions for the operator's full DISPLAY NAME (#J-002).
78	
79	    The username/email rules leave the real name (e.g. a git ``user.name`` like "[REDACTED]")
80	    in cleartext — distinct operator PII that appears in git author strings, ``pyproject``
81	    ``authors=``, and prose, often right beside an already-redacted email. Derive candidate names
82	    from ``git config user.name`` and the ``ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES`` env (comma-separated), redact each
83	    (and, for a multi-word name, its whitespace-flexible form). Length-guarded to avoid
84	    over-redacting common words.
85	    """
86	    names: list[str] = []
87	    extra = os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES", "")
88	    names.extend(n.strip() for n in extra.split(",") if n.strip())
89	    # Derive the operator name from SEVERAL sources — any one may be empty in a given context
90	    # (#J-002 follow-up): on this node local `git config user.name` is unset yet commits still
91	    # carry an author via global config / env, so a single-source lookup scrubbed nothing. Cover
92	    # the env author vars, git config, AND the actual last-commit author.
93	    for env_name in ("GIT_AUTHOR_NAME", "GIT_COMMITTER_NAME"):
94	        v = os.environ.get(env_name, "").strip()
95	        if v:
96	            names.append(v)
97	    try:
98	        import subprocess
99	
100	        for cmd in (["git", "config", "user.name"], ["git", "log", "-1", "--format=%an"]):
101	            out = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
102	            if out.returncode == 0 and out.stdout.strip():
103	                names.append(out.stdout.strip())
104	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - git absent/misconfigured must never break capture
105	        pass
106	    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
107	    seen: set[str] = set()
108	    for n in names:
109	        if len(n) < 4 or n in seen:
110	            continue  # too short → risks over-redacting common words; or already added
111	        seen.add(n)
112	        # Flexible inter-token whitespace (e.g. "[REDACTED]"). NO leading \b: the name often
113	        # appears glued to a JSON-escaped newline ("...---\\n[REDACTED]") where \b doesn't fire
114	        # because the escape's 'n' abuts the name (#J-002 follow-up). A trailing \b still prevents
115	        # matching inside a longer word (e.g. "Joneses"); the >=4-char guard bounds over-redaction.
116	        toks = n.split()
117	        pats.append(re.compile(r"\s+".join(re.escape(t) for t in toks) + r"\b"))
118	        # Redact each NAME TOKEN standalone too (#J-002 confirm-concern): the full-name pattern
119	        # leaves the operator's bare first/last name in cleartext — e.g. "[REDACTED] approves ..." and
120	        # "[REDACTED] builds and governs ..." appear in governance prose hundreds of times, uniquely
121	        # re-identifying the operator. Per-token redaction (len>=4, trailing \b, no leading \b for
122	        # the JSON-\n-glued case) closes it. Over-redaction is bounded: the tokens come only from
123	        # the verified operator name and the corpus contains no unrelated bearer of these tokens.
124	        for t in toks:
125	            if len(t) >= 4 and t not in seen:
126	                seen.add(t)
127	                pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(t) + r"\b"))
128	    return pats
129	
130	
131	def _repo_handle_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
132	    """Redactions for the operator's repo owner / GitHub handle (#J-008).
133	
134	    The handle (the ``<owner>`` in ``github.com/<owner>/<repo>``, e.g. in PR/issue URLs and
135	    ``gh --repo <owner>/...`` commands) is operator-identifying PII that NO existing rule covers:
136	    it is not an email (the email rule needs an ``@``), and it differs from the home-dir username
137	    the bare-username rule derives. Left uncovered it leaked across hundreds of transcript files
138	    (#J-008). Derive candidate handles from the git remotes and the ``ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES`` env;
139	    redact each as a bare token (length-guarded). A handle is a single unambiguous string, so no
140	    word-boundary is needed — redact it inside URLs, ``gh`` args, and the ``<handle>@`` email stem.
141	    """
142	    handles: list[str] = []
143	    extra = os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES", "")
144	    handles.extend(h.strip() for h in extra.split(",") if h.strip())
145	    try:
146	        import subprocess
147	
148	        out = subprocess.run(["git", "remote", "-v"], capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
149	        if out.returncode == 0:
150	            for m in re.finditer(r"github\.com[:/]([^/\s]+)/", out.stdout):
151	                handles.append(m.group(1))
152	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - git absent/misconfigured must never break capture
153	        pass
154	    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
155	    seen: set[str] = set()
156	    for h in handles:
157	        if len(h) < 4 or h in seen:
158	            continue  # too short → risks over-redacting common words; or already added
159	        seen.add(h)
160	        pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(h)))
161	    return pats
162	
163	
164	_USERNAME_RE = _username_pattern()
165	_DISPLAY_NAME_RES = _display_name_patterns()
166	_REPO_HANDLE_RES = _repo_handle_patterns()
167	
168	
169	def _scrub(text: str) -> str:
170	    for pat in _SECRET_PATTERNS:
171	        if pat.groups == 2:
172	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]\2", text)
173	        elif pat.groups == 1:
174	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]", text)
175	        else:
176	            text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
177	    # Redact the bare operator username everywhere (#G-007), after the path rule so masked paths
178	    # don't re-expose it. Done last so it also catches it inside project-dir slugs / git authors.
179	    if _USERNAME_RE is not None:
180	        text = _USERNAME_RE.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
181	    # Redact the operator's full display name + bare name tokens too (#J-002) — distinct PII the
182	    # username/email rules leave in cleartext (git author strings, pyproject authors=, prose).
183	    for pat in _DISPLAY_NAME_RES:
184	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
185	    # Redact the repo owner / GitHub handle (#J-008) — operator-identifying PII in PR/issue URLs
186	    # and `gh --repo <handle>/...` commands that no email/username rule covers.
187	    for pat in _REPO_HANDLE_RES:
188	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
189	    return text
190	
191	
192	def _active_session(src: Path) -> Path | None:
193	    """The most-recently-modified top-level transcript = the live session."""
194	    candidates = sorted(src.glob("*.jsonl"), key=lambda p: p.stat().st_mtime, reverse=True)
195	    return candidates[0] if candidates else None
196	
197	
198	def _copy_scrubbed(srcf: Path, dstf: Path) -> dict | None:
199	    try:
200	        dstf.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
201	        raw = srcf.read_bytes()
202	        # Scrub EVERY text file, not only .jsonl (#CV-O-1): the session subtree also holds .json
203	        # workflow/subagent transcripts that carry the same operator PII + credential-shaped
204	        # secrets — the old suffix==".jsonl" gate copied those RAW, bypassing redaction entirely.
205	        #
206	        # FAIL CLOSED on redaction (#J-001): classify binary by an actual binary SIGNAL (a NUL
207	        # byte), NOT by UTF-8 validity. A transcript routinely embeds arbitrary subprocess stdout
208	        # (latin-1 dumps, truncated multibyte) inside JSONL strings; gating on strict decode meant
209	        # a single stray byte routed the WHOLE file to the verbatim branch and re-leaked it (the
210	        # CV-O-1 fix had turned an always-redact path into a fail-open one). Decode with
211	        # errors="replace" so redaction ALWAYS runs on text; only a genuinely binary blob (NUL
212	        # present) is copied verbatim.
213	        if b"\x00" in raw:
214	            data = raw  # genuinely binary — nothing to scrub
215	        else:
216	            data = _scrub(raw.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")).encode("utf-8")
217	        if dstf.exists() and dstf.read_bytes() == data:
218	            pass  # unchanged — still report it in the manifest
219	        else:
220	            dstf.write_bytes(data)
221	        return {
222	            "path": str(dstf.relative_to(DEST.parent.parent)),
223	            "bytes": len(data),
224	            "sha256": hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest(),
225	        }
226	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 — provenance capture must never break a commit
227	        return None
228	
229	
230	def main() -> int:
231	    src = Path(os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC", str(DEFAULT_SRC)))
232	    if not src.is_dir():
233	        return 0
234	    session = _active_session(src)
235	    if session is None:
236	        return 0
237	    sid = session.stem
238	    captured: list[dict] = []
239	
240	    rec = _copy_scrubbed(session, DEST / f"{sid}.jsonl")
241	    if rec:
242	        captured.append(rec)
243	
244	    # the session's subtree: subagents/ + workflows/ transcripts
245	    subtree = src / sid
246	    if subtree.is_dir():
247	        for f in sorted(subtree.rglob("*")):
248	            if f.is_file():
249	                rel = f.relative_to(subtree)
250	                rec = _copy_scrubbed(f, DEST / sid / rel)
251	                if rec:
252	                    captured.append(rec)
253	
254	    try:
255	        MANIFEST.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
256	        MANIFEST.write_text(
257	            json.dumps(
258	                {
259	                    "captured_at": _dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc).isoformat(),
260	                    "active_session": sid,
261	                    "n_files": len(captured),
262	                    "total_bytes": sum(c["bytes"] for c in captured),
263	                    "files": captured,
264	                },
265	                indent=2,
266	            )
267	            + "\n",
268	            encoding="utf-8",
269	        )
270	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
271	        return 0
272	    print(f"captured {len(captured)} transcript file(s) for session {sid}")
273	    return 0
274	
275	
276	if __name__ == "__main__":
277	    sys.exit(main())
278

7c50440 fix(hardening-p7): close operator-identity PII residue — bare name tokens + GitHub handle (J-002 concern, J-008)
00b9df9 fix(hardening-p7): scrub operator name even when glued to a JSON \n escape (J-002 follow-up)
d6a7896 fix(hardening-p7): make the J-002 display-name scrub actually derive a name on this node
harden/pass-7

content.text: Now let me check the tests and run the corpus verification greps.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && echo "=== [REDACTED] (any) ===" && grep -rl "[REDACTED]" transcripts/ | wc -l && echo "=== [REDACTED] ===" && grep -rl "[REDACTED]" transcripts/ | wc -l && echo "=== [REDACTED] not Joneses ===" && grep -rloE "[REDACTED][^e]" transcripts/ | wc -l && echo "=== [REDACTED] (username) ===" && grep -rl "[REDACTED]" transcripts/ | wc -l
input.description: Grep corpus for operator identity tokens

content.text: Corpus greps are clean. Let me read the tests and run them, plus check for other residual operator-identity vectors.

content.content:
140	    cap = _load("scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "capture_transcripts_fc2")
141	    src = tmp_path / "t.jsonl"
142	    src.write_bytes(b'{"k":"[REDACTED] \x80 end"}')  # invalid \x80
143	    dst = tmp_path / "out" / "t.jsonl"
144	    cap._copy_scrubbed(src, dst)
145	    out = dst.read_bytes()
146	    assert b"abcdefghijklmnopqrstuv" not in out and b"[REDACTED]" in out
147	
148	
149	def test_scrub_redacts_operator_display_name(monkeypatch):
150	    # #J-002: the operator's full display name (from ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES / git user.name) is redacted,
151	    # not just the email — distinct PII that leaked beside redacted emails.
152	    monkeypatch.setenv("ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES", "Ada Lovelace")
153	    cap = _load("scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "capture_transcripts_name")
154	    out = cap._scrub("Author: Ada Lovelace <[REDACTED]>; also Ada   Lovelace in prose")
155	    assert "Ada Lovelace" not in out and "Ada   Lovelace" not in out  # flexible whitespace too
156	    assert "[REDACTED]" not in out  # email still scrubbed
157	
158	
159	def test_scrub_redacts_bare_name_tokens(monkeypatch):
160	    # #J-002 confirm-concern: the operator's bare first/last name (not just the full name) is
161	    # operator PII — "[REDACTED] approves ..." appears in governance prose hundreds of times. Each
162	    # name token (len>=4) is redacted standalone, while a longer word containing it is protected.
163	    monkeypatch.setenv("ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES", "[REDACTED]")
164	    cap = _load("scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "capture_transcripts_tokens")
165	    out = cap._scrub("[REDACTED] approves the charter; [REDACTED] governs. The Joneses are unrelated.")
166	    assert "[REDACTED] approves" not in out  # bare first name redacted
167	    assert "[REDACTED] governs" not in out  # bare last name redacted

189	    assert b"example-study-secret-key".decode() not in src  # the stale weak literal is gone
190	    assert "os.urandom(32)" in src  # the fresh-per-run secret is in place

content.text:
Significant findings. The fix has real residual gaps. Let me confirm the key leaks.

The grep reveals operator-identity PII still in the corpus that the fix's token/handle patterns should have caught but did NOT:

1. `"initiated_by": "[REDACTED]@hcgrc"` — lowercase "[REDACTED]" as an email-stem. The display-name token pattern uses `re.escape(t) + r"\b"` with case-sensitive matching, so "[REDACTED]\b" does NOT match lowercase "[REDACTED]". This is the operator's first name in cleartext.
2. `[REDACTED]-[REDACTED]` (human) — lowercase, hyphenated, not matched.
3. `[REDACTED]-audience-profiles` — the handle pattern derives from `git remote` (`[REDACTED]`), but here the leaked token is the bare `[REDACTED]` fragment in a different compound. Let me verify whether `[REDACTED]` itself survives anywhere and whether these are genuine leaks vs. meta-discussion.

content.text:
Confirmed. Now let me determine whether the test would fail if reverted (discrimination), and run the test suite. The remote owner is `[REDACTED]`, so the handle pattern redacts `[REDACTED]` but NOT the bare `[REDACTED]` substring (it uses `re.escape(h)` for the full handle, no sub-token). `[REDACTED]-audience-profiles` is a skill name containing the handle-minus-prefix — arguably a genuine identity-adjacent leak, though distinct from the full handle.

The "[REDACTED]@hcgrc" / "[REDACTED]-[REDACTED]" lowercase leaks are the more serious finding: the case-sensitive name-token pattern misses the lowercased first name.

Tests pass. Let me verify the regression tests are discriminating by checking they'd fail under revert (the token pattern is the new code; without it the bare-name test would fail since the full-name pattern "[REDACTED]" wouldn't match "[REDACTED] approves").

The tests are discriminating by construction: `test_scrub_redacts_bare_name_tokens` asserts "[REDACTED] approves" is gone, which only the new per-token loop achieves; `test_scrub_redacts_github_handle` asserts the handle is gone, only the new `_repo_handle_patterns` achieves. Both would fail if the respective new code were reverted.

But the committed corpus is NOT clean. Let me do a final confirmation of the email-stem case and whether the fix author claimed the corpus was clean.

input.description: Final confirmation of residual lowercase identity tokens

content.text:
Confirmed conclusively. The corpus is NOT clean. There are lowercase operator-identity tokens surviving in the committed corpus:

- `[REDACTED]@hcgrc` (4 files) — operator first name as an email stem (note: not caught by the email rule because `@hcgrc` has no TLD dot, and not caught by the case-sensitive `[REDACTED]\b` token pattern).
- `[REDACTED]-[REDACTED]` (human) (1 file) — lowercase hyphenated full name.
- Standalone lowercase `[REDACTED]` in 6+ files (some are meta-discussion of the scrub itself, but `wf_82001839/agent-a61...` is `[REDACTED]-[REDACTED]` in a real "human" actor table, and `wf_cdf1b8e2` instances need the same scrutiny).
- `[REDACTED]-audience-profiles` skill name (8 files) — the handle-derived identity fragment.

Many of these are case-insensitivity gaps: the pattern is `re.escape(t) + r"\b"` with no `re.IGNORECASE`, so the lowercased first name in identifiers (`[REDACTED]@hcgrc`, `[REDACTED]-[REDACTED]`) survives. Some `[REDACTED]` hits are legitimate meta-discussion of the redaction logic itself ("Joneses safe", "the Joneses live here", regex comments) — those are not PII. But `[REDACTED]-[REDACTED] (human)` in an actor table and `[REDACTED]@hcgrc` as an initiated_by field are genuine, re-identifying operator PII.

Let me make my determination.

input.rationale:
The fix targets the right root cause at the right layer (scripts/capture_transcripts.py _scrub), and the approach is sound: per-token name redaction (#J-002 confirm-concern) and a new _repo_handle_patterns deriving the owner from git remote + ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES (#J-008). The regression tests are discriminating — test_scrub_redacts_bare_name_tokens asserts "[REDACTED] approves" is gone (only the new per-token loop achieves this; the full-name "[REDACTED]" pattern would not match), and test_scrub_redacts_github_handle asserts the handle is gone (only the new pattern achieves this); both would fail if reverted. All 13 tests pass. The over-redaction trade-off is acceptable (token guard len>=4, trailing \b protects "Joneses"). The capitalized targets are clean: grep for "[REDACTED]"=0, "[REDACTED]"=0, "[REDACTED][^e]"=0, "[REDACTED]"=0.

However the committed corpus is NOT actually clean — the completeness claim fails. The name-token and handle patterns are CASE-SENSITIVE (re.escape(t) + r"\b", no re.IGNORECASE), so lowercased operator-identity tokens survived the in-place re-scrub. Concrete residuals (verified by grep, excluding self-referential meta-discussion of the scrub logic): "[REDACTED]@hcgrc" as an initiated_by field in 4 files (also dodges the email rule because "@hcgrc" has no TLD dot), "[REDACTED]-[REDACTED] (human)" in an actor table, standalone lowercase "[REDACTED]" in a real actor table, and the handle-derived fragment "[REDACTED]-audience-profiles" (skill name) in 8 files. The lowercase first name + hyphenated full name re-identify the operator just as the capitalized forms did — the same class of PII the fix was meant to close, just in a different case.
concerns: Case-sensitivity gap: name-token and full-name patterns use re.escape(t)+r'\b' with no re.IGNORECASE, so the lowercased operator first name '[REDACTED]' survives in the committed corpus — '[REDACTED]@hcgrc' appears as an initiated_by field in 4 transcript files (transcripts/sessions/8d54714a-.../subagents/workflows/wf_82001839-351/agent-a18107c97bdfa2626.jsonl and 3 others). This is the same first-name PII the #J-002 confirm-concern flagged, just lowercased.

Lowercase hyphenated full name '[REDACTED]-[REDACTED]' (human) survives in an actor table in transcripts/.../wf_82001839-351/agent-a61cd08ca3c6d93a2.jsonl, plus standalone lowercase '[REDACTED]' in real (non-meta) actor-table contexts in wf_574c42b7 and wf_cdf1b8e2 transcripts — not matched by the case-sensitive token pattern.

'[REDACTED]@hcgrc' is not covered by the email rule either, because the email regex requires a '\.[A-Za-z]{2,}' TLD and 'hcgrc' has no dot — so neither the email rule nor the new name-token rule redacts this operator email-stem.

Handle-fragment leak: the handle pattern redacts the full remote owner '[REDACTED]' but not the bare substring '[REDACTED]', leaving '[REDACTED]-audience-profiles' (a skill name embedding the handle minus its 'the' prefix) in cleartext across 8 transcript files. Borderline identity-adjacent rather than a hard handle leak, but it is the operator handle stem.

Recommended fix: add re.IGNORECASE to the display-name token patterns (or lowercase-normalize), and add a TLD-less email-stem rule or include '[REDACTED]' as a case-insensitive token; then re-scrub the corpus in place again and re-run the same greps to confirm zero (excluding the scrub's own self-referential meta-discussion of 'Joneses'/'the Joneses live here').

content.text:
I've completed the adversarial review. Verdict: CONCERN.

The fix is appropriate (right root cause, right layer, discriminating regression tests, all 13 tests pass) but the committed corpus is NOT clean. The capitalized targets are all zero, but case-sensitivity gaps left lowercased operator-identity PII in place:

- `[REDACTED]@hcgrc` (initiated_by field, 4 files) — also dodges the email rule since `@hcgrc` has no TLD dot
- `[REDACTED]-[REDACTED] (human)` and standalone lowercase `[REDACTED]` in real actor tables
- `[REDACTED]-audience-profiles` (8 files) — handle stem

The core defect is that `_display_name_patterns` / `_repo_handle_patterns` compile patterns without `re.IGNORECASE`. Re-scrubbing with case-insensitive matching (plus a TLD-less email-stem rule) and re-running the greps would close it.
